student_response: Before the 1790s, the new republic had a written frame of government, debts from the struggle for independence, farms, ports, merchants, and arguments about public power. Citizens read newspapers and attended meetings. The country was young and its leaders talked about what a republic should look like. During the decade, Hamiltonian ideas and Jeffersonian ideas were discussed in the capital and in local communities. Hamiltonian statements mentioned federal officials, public credit, commerce, and national direction. Jeffersonian statements mentioned farmers, local voters, state authority, and a limited central office. These were political views in the early republic. They appeared in speeches, letters, and newspapers, and people repeated them in many forms. The First Bank of the United States was chartered in 1791. It held federal deposits, sold stock, handled money, and kept accounts. Its directors recorded payments and shares. The Kentucky and Virginia Resolutions appeared in 1798 and 1799. They stated ideas about states and federal laws. The texts discussed powers claimed by state and national officials. Political clubs and newspaper editors also used harsh words. Elections had accusations, rumors, and personal attacks. Federal power was a major subject. Farming was another subject. Trade was talked about too. The visions had positions about the nation, its economy, and its goverment. Voters heard these positions in public discussion. Details were often confusing or misspeled.

The republic entered the 1790s with war debts, a new federal framework, growing ports, wide farming districts, and public debate over the meaning of representative government. Newspapers printed speeches and attacks. Voters heard many plans for the country. Hamilton envisioned a nation directed through federal finance and commercial policy, and Jefferson envisioned a nation centered on independent farmers and restricted national authority. Their programs covered money, offices, diplomacy, taxes, land, and the role of ordinary citizens. Supporters gathered in political groups. Editors used partisan labels. Public meetings could become loud and angry. Federal officers worked in the capital, and state officials handled many local matters. The decade had elections and cabinet disputes. Two items often listed for this period are the First Bank of the United States and the Kentucky and Virginia Resolutions. The First Bank of the United States received a charter in 1791. It held deposits and issued shares. Merchants and goverment officers knew its name. There were arguments in print about its charter. The Kentucky and Virginia Resolutions were documents from 1798 and 1799. The documents included statements about states and national laws. Copies and summaries circulated among political readers. The decade also had many general complaints about elite officeholders, rural interests, city interests, taxes, and personal freedom. People sometimes called opponents dangerous. Some writers feared disorder. Some writers feared concentrated office. These statements filled papers and pamphlets. Political writing used the two labels. Federal programs appeared in many statements. Agrarian programs appeared in many statements. National office was a repeated topic.

Before independence, household law placed most formal authority with men, plantation labor used hereditary bondage, and political writing discussed natural rights and consent. Religious sermons, pamphlets, family rules, and colonial assemblies contained ideas about duty and liberty. The effects of revolutionary ideals were a narrow civic expectation for women and no recognized liberty for enslaved African Americans. Women were discussed as members of households and as teachers of children. Their reading and moral conduct received attention. Formal voting and officeholding were usually male activities. Marriage law put property and legal identity under husbands in many cases. Daily work in homes, shops, and farms. Often important, rarely listed as political authority. Some girls received education in reading and household subjects. General praise for virtue. General limits in law. Enslaved people heard liberty language in towns, ports, farms, and churches. Bondage was written into property records and labor rules. Families faced sale and separation. Work under commands, patrols, and punishment. Freedom petitions appeared in some courts, and private emancipation occurred in some households. Enslaved people had no ballot, office, or secure legal control over family life. Political vocabulary included equality, rights, consent, liberty, virtue, citizenship, and representation. Legal records included property claims, marriage rules, voting requirements, registrations, petitions, and manumissions. Republican Motherhood was a phrase about womens civic duty inside the family. It included education, virtue, motherhood, and the raising of future citizens. The phrase appeared in discussions of female learning. No public office in the phrase itself. The Pennsylvania Gradual Abolition Act was passed in 1780. Its text listed registrations, births, and age rules. A named law in one state. Public writing contained broad statements about equality. Womens legal status included restrictions. Ownership records classified enslaved African Americans as property in much of the country. Free Black communities existed in towns. White households often expected female obedience. Political participation had firm boundaries. Household manuals discussed obedience and child raising. Property documents listed people, land, tools, and debts. Court papers recorded claims. Church meetings included free and enslaved worshipers. Newspapers printed notices about sales and escapes. Schools admitted some girls. Offices and ballots belonged to men under general practice. These are pieces of the period. Many statements about freedom. Many rules about status. Each state kept statutes and customs. Fragmented records and unsupported promises about freedom for everybody.

Northern factory labor was organized around paid work, set hours, supervisors, and machines, and southern plantation labor was organized around forced work, owner authority, field routines, and no free wage agreement. That was the basic division in labor organization during these years. Factory workers entered buildings, followed bells or daily schedules, did repeated jobs, and received pay, the workday could be long and the rules could be strict and workers had little control during the shift. Plantation workers lived under permanent commands, labor groups were assigned tasks, overseers watched the work, and punishment was part of the system, families also lived under the owners control. The northern system had contracts and wages in general terms, the southern system had ownership and coercion in general terms. Factory employees could leave a job at some point. Enslaved plantation workers were not legally free to leave. Workers followed routines, discipline, and production goals. This is how labor was set up in the period, the exact years and rules were unclear to many workers and the schedule contained long days.
